title: The CBI Must Investigate the Vishnupriya Case!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil reading copy below repairs only source-confirmed OCR defects, including damaged word joins in the account of Vishnupriya's lottery-shop enforcement. Allegations, suspicions, and quoted claims remain attributed exactly as they are in the letter. The canonical Tamil source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: Yesterday morning, 18 March 2016, Kazhagam Treasurer, younger brother M.K. Stalin, brought to meet me Mr M. Ravi, the beloved father of police officer Vishnupriya, the Deputy Superintendent of Police at Tiruchengode, who died under mysterious circumstances six months ago, along with some of her close relatives. As soon as I saw them, they all spoke with one voice: “Vishnupriya's death was not a suicide. She was murdered. The truth about it must become known to the world. You have therefore insisted that this incident be investigated by the CBI, but the Tamil Nadu Government has not listened.” One after another, they levelled allegations against this Government. I consoled them before they left.
Vishnupriya belonged to the Dalit community. In particular, she was the first police officer to secure employment through the internal reservation for Arunthathiyars.

According to National Crime Records Bureau documents, there were 58 suicides among police personnel in Tamil Nadu in 2012—the highest number in India—and 31 police personnel died by suicide in 2013. In other words, Tamil Nadu recorded the largest number of such suicides in India after Maharashtra. Among the significant police suicides in Tamil Nadu over the past five years were those of Mylapore Law and Order Inspector Rajasekar; Head Constable Nagaiasamy in Madurai; women Sub-Inspectors Esther and Renganayaki in Madurai; Sub-Inspector Muthiah in Avadi; Inspector Ravichandran in St Thomas Mount; and Karuppayi in Nagalapuram.
At the time of Vishnupriya's death, *Junior Vikatan* wrote:

> “If even a DSP can be subjected to such torment, this incident is an example of what these police officers might do to ordinary members of the public. Vishnupriya was placed under restrictions: she had to do whatever she was told and, whatever the reason, she was forbidden from doing anything that caused inconvenience to the person issuing the commands. She was treated like a slave. She was demeaned because she was a woman and subjected to still greater humiliation because she was a Dalit. Who said that an educated woman would no longer face humiliation, or that Dalits would gain respect merely by reaching positions of authority? This death shows that society's sharp claw will tear a person apart wherever that person may be.”
Those words deserve profound consideration.

Immediately after DSP Vishnupriya died, every opposition party in Tamil Nadu, including the DMK, asked the Tamil Nadu Government to arrange a CBI investigation. But Chief Minister Jayalalithaa hastily rejected a CBI inquiry and announced that an investigation by the State Government-controlled CB-CID would suffice. Reports are now emerging one after another. First, it was said that several pages were missing from the letter written by Vishnupriya. Then it was said that the signature in the letter was not hers; that the letter itself had not been written by her; and that her important camera was missing. Reports raising suspicion have also emerged about the place where she was said to have hanged herself and about her feet having remained on the floor when she was found hanging.
Following Vishnupriya's death, her close friend Maheswari, who serves as DSP at Kilakarai in Ramanathapuram district, told journalists in Salem:

> “At about 2.48 p.m. on Friday, Vishnupriya called me and was speaking to me. She then said that the Namakkal District Superintendent of Police was calling on another line and disconnected my call. After that, Vishnupriya's mobile phone was switched off. Only later did I learn that she had died by suicide. The Gokulraj murder case was not properly investigated. Vishnupriya told me that, under pressure from senior officers, some people unconnected with the case were arrested and imprisoned, and that she was also being compelled to register cases against them under the Goondas Act. Vishnupriya was an honest officer. She was driven to suicide by pressure from senior officers, by their addressing her disrespectfully in the singular, and by their insulting her as someone unfit for police work. The investigation must be transferred to the CBI. Action must be taken to suspend the senior officers.”
DSP Maheswari gave this detailed account at the time itself.
Newspapers also reported at the time:

> “From the beginning of the engineer Gokulraj murder case, allegations arose that senior officers were helping the principal accused to escape. Only by producing false accused persons can the real culprits be allowed to escape. It is therefore said that a senior Chennai-based officer holding the rank of ADGP repeatedly pressured the SP and DSP in this case to act as instructed. It is not known whether anyone else pressured the ADGP. But there may be a large conspiracy behind this. In particular, it is said that politicians in power may be connected to it. Consequently, there is intense discussion even within police circles that the case must be transferred to the CBI.”

Ravi also expressed his anguish that allegations had been made against Vishnupriya's superior, Namakkal Superintendent of Police S.R. Senthil Kumar, yet the officer was not transferred from there. He said that his daughter had handled several other cases in addition to the Gokulraj murder case and had faced many threats while investigating them. His wife had told the investigating officers what Vishnupriya had said to her about those threats, but no investigation was conducted into the matter.
Former DGP Thilagavathi said:

> “Vishnupriya's death is shrouded in mystery. The truth will emerge only at the end of an investigation. A CB-CID inquiry has been constituted into DSP Vishnupriya's suicide. It will not be impartial, because it is a police agency under the Tamil Nadu Government. The truth will emerge only if a CBI investigation is instituted.”
When someone who herself served as DGP states that an inquiry by the State CB-CID will not be impartial and will not reveal the truth, fair-minded people must accept the force of that statement.

Yesterday, Vishnupriya's father and relatives met me at my home in Chennai and personally recounted several important incidents. Her father told me that no family problem had caused his daughter's death. He reminded me of what he had stated at the time:

> “We will not receive Vishnupriya's body until a CBI investigation is ordered. My daughter was not a coward who would take her own life. Senior officers are responsible for her death. Vishnupriya had said that she faced threats from the accused connected to the Gokulraj murder case and pressure from senior officers concerning that case. The police are claiming that there was a family problem only to divert the case. The police showed us only four pages of the letter she wrote; they concealed the remaining pages. The police have seized Vishnupriya's laptop, two mobile phones and camera. There is a possibility that they may attempt to destroy the evidence on the mobile phones. We have no confidence in the police investigation of this case. It must therefore be transferred to the CBI.”

Vishnupriya's father Ravi further lamented that, although allegations had been made against her superior, Namakkal Superintendent of Police S.R. Senthil Kumar, the officer had not been transferred. He said that his daughter had handled several other cases in addition to the Gokulraj murder case and had faced many threats while investigating them. His wife had told the investigating officers what Vishnupriya had said to her about those threats, but no inquiry was conducted into the matter.

As DSP of Tiruchengode, Vishnupriya was responsible for eliminating the one-number lottery in the Tiruchengode area. Indeed, she deserves credit for raiding lottery shops, arresting several people and having them remanded. She also conducted inspections at TASMAC shops against the wishes of Namakkal SP Senthil Kumar. To tell the truth, because she inspected those shops, she was made to wait in the office from morning until evening; the SP severely reprimanded Vishnupriya for having taken action according to law. This led to a confrontation between them.

Vishnupriya was also a supervising officer in the important murder case involving mill owner Jegannathan. She had made recordings related to that case on a video camera in her possession. That camera, along with a laptop, two mobile phones, and other important devices, was taken away by SP Senthil Kumar on the evening of the very day Vishnupriya died, the 18th. Vishnupriya had stored on her camera and laptop a great deal of important information concerning the cases she investigated. Her father also said that ten pen drives his daughter had kept securely were missing. With anguish, he told me that no inquiry whatsoever had been conducted into these missing items.

He also raised the question of who had examined his daughter's mobile phone at midnight. He said that, on the night it was taken away, someone had operated Vishnupriya's phone, a fact detected through the mobile phone of her friend, Kilakarai DSP Maheswari. He further said that a WhatsApp message an IAS officer sent to his daughter confirmed that someone had inspected her phone, and he demanded an intensive investigation into all these matters.

Under Jayalalithaa's rule, official suicides and efforts to conceal and divert the truth about them without the slightest conscience have continued without end. When everyone insisted that this incident be investigated by the CBI, the very haste with which the AIADMK Government announced a CB-CID investigation makes one suspect that an effort to hide the truth in this case had already begun.

Ravi asked why Namakkal Additional SP Xavier Beski was transferred immediately to Chennai after Vishnupriya died. He said that those who feared his presence there would pose a danger to the handling of the case had transferred him at once, and he questioned why the transfer was carried out with such urgency.

Ravi strongly maintained that the room in which his daughter was said to have hanged herself was only twelve feet high; that the arch from which she was said to have hanged herself was set within the wall; and that neither a rope nor a sari could be threaded through it. An independent and impartial investigation beyond the control of the State Government was therefore essential to determine whether his daughter's death was suicide or murder. Only then, he said, would he learn the truth about her death.
Even in the petition he gave me, he poured out his anguish:

> “The Tamil Nadu Government attempted only to portray my daughter's death as suicide. Through the combined efforts of the Tamil Nadu Government and senior police officers, evidence has been destroyed and the truth covered up. When senior police officers themselves are involved in this death, how can we obtain justice from a government that protects officials who act against justice? I pressed the Tamil Nadu Government to order a CBI investigation into this matter and subsequently submitted a formal petition. To this day, our family has received no justice of any kind from the Tamil Nadu Government.”

The views expressed by Vishnupriya's father and the others about this case cannot be dismissed. Under Jayalalithaa's rule, official suicides and efforts to conceal and divert the truth about them without the slightest conscience have continued without end. When everyone insisted that this incident be investigated by the CBI, the very haste with which the AIADMK Government announced a CB-CID investigation makes one suspect that an effort to hide the truth in this case had already begun.

Therefore, as many different parties have demanded, this case must be transferred to the CBI so that justice may be secured in Vishnupriya's death and the true facts made known to the country. If the Government delays or fails to do so, the many suspicions already present among the people concerning Vishnupriya's death will only be confirmed; they will not diminish in the slightest!
With affection,
M.K.
